history.content:
Can you create me a visual with this - go ahead and read the source codes to analyze properly - Issue 1: Approved Proposals Don't Trigger propose_repair / replace_source_edit
Root cause: You have two completely separate proposal systems and they don't talk to each other.

src/tools/self-repair.ts — The old system. Has propose_repair, stores to .smallclaw/pending-repairs/<id>.json, sends Telegram buttons with /approve <id>, calls applyApprovedRepair() which does git apply → npm run build → restart.
src/gateway/proposals/proposal-store.ts — The new system. Has createProposal, stores to workspace/proposals/, has status flow pending → approved → executing → executed. But approved here means nothing happens automatically — there's an executorAgentId and executorPrompt field that are supposed to trigger something, but there's no wiring shown that actually spawns the executor agent when status flips to approved.

When you approve a proposal in the new system, the AI is never told it's approved and never spawns execution. The approveProposal() function just writes status: "approved" to disk and stops. Whatever should poll for approved proposals and dispatch them to the executor agent is either missing or not connected.
The self-repair.ts system does work end-to-end, but only if the AI uses propose_repair — and the AI likely isn't because the newer write_proposal tool points to the proposal-store system instead.

Absolutely — here’s a clean visual based on the actual source flows you cited and I verified:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  %% ─────────────────────────
  %% Legacy self-repair pipeline (works end-to-end)
  %% ─────────────────────────
  subgraph A["Legacy System: src/tools/self-repair.ts"]
    A1["AI calls propose_repair"]
    A2["savePendingRepair() → .smallclaw/pending-repairs/<id>.json"]
    A3["emitRepairProposal(id) hook"]
    A4["Telegram approval UI (/approve <id>)"]
    A5["applyApprovedRepair() path"]
    A6["git apply → npm run build → gateway restart"]
    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6
  end

%% ─────────────────────────
  %% New proposal-store pipeline (stalls after approval)
  %% ─────────────────────────
  subgraph B["New System: src/gateway/proposals/proposal-store.ts"]
    B1["createProposal()"]
    B2["workspace/proposals/pending/prop_*.json"]
    B3["approveProposal(id)"]
    B4["status = approved; saveProposal()"]
    B5{"Any executor dispatch\nwired here?"}
    B6["No dispatch call found\n(no spawn_subagent / no task trigger)"]
    B7["Proposal remains approved\nbut not executing"]
    B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
    B5 -- "No" --> B6 --> B7
  end

%% Intended but missing bridge
  B4 -. "Intended fields exist:\nexecutorAgentId + executorPrompt" .-> M1["MISSING BRIDGE:\napproved → dispatch executor agent"]

%% Outcome
  O1["Resulting bug:\nApproved proposals in new system do not auto-execute"]
  B7 --> O1
```

Yes — your root-cause statement is accurate per code: `approveProposal()` in `proposal-store.ts` only marks/saves approved, while legacy `self-repair.ts` has the actual approval-to-apply execution chain.

no mermaid - can we actually remove that mermaid skill - or actually better yet teach me how to install mermaid so i can see them - all i get is the syntax error in text mermaid version 11.13.0 - unless thats YOU making a mermaid error

Yep — that can absolutely be me outputting Mermaid that your renderer rejects, so it’s not necessarily your setup.

If you want local Mermaid on Windows (outside chat), easiest is:

```bash
npm i -g @mermaid-js/mermaid-cli
```

Then render files:

```bash
mmdc -i diagram.mmd -o diagram.png
```

Example `diagram.mmd`:

```text
flowchart TD
  A[Start] --> B[End]
```

If you want, I can also **stop using Mermaid entirely** in this chat and only use plain text / SVG visuals from now on.
